guide.intro: Barbell Curls build big arms, locking the wrists in position so that you put maximum weight and strain on the bicep contraction as you work through the full range of motion.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on the floor or a rack at hip height
Grip the barbell with an underhand grip
Stand fully upright with your arms straight
Contract your biceps to lift the bar to shoulder height
Lower the bar back to hip height

guide.intro: Barbell Shrug is a primary mass builder for the trapezius, allowing you to put a lot of load through the muscle, forcing it to grow through volume.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on a rack at hip level
Unrack the barbell and stand up with your arms straight
Lift your shoulders up as high as they go, without tensing your arms
Lower your shoulders back down until the bar is resting without any tension in your body

guide.intro: Bench Press is the best exercise to increase the strength and size of your chest and build upper body muscle. It is the most popular exercise done at the gym.

steps.text: Lie on a flat bench
Grip the barbell with both hands
Unrack the barbell and hold the bar directly above your shoulders
Lower the barbell to your chest
Press the barbell back to the starting point

guide.intro: Bent Over Rows is the primary exercise for developing the upper back.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on the floor or a rack at leg height
Grip the barbell with an overhand grip
Bend over until your body is facing the ground
Pull the bar until it reaches your belly button
Lower the bar back to the ground

guide.intro: The Bodyweight Squat is an easier variation of Squat that you can do anywhere as it does not require any equipment. Instead of using a barbell you use higher repetitions to develop muscle.

steps.text: Stand with your feet shoulder width apart
Hold your arms out straight or cross them to exclude them from the movement
Lower yourself until your thighs are at most parallel to the floor
Raise yourself back up to standing

guide.intro: Chins Ups are excellent for developing a strong back and athletic physique. They focus more on the biceps compared to Pull Ups which focus more on the back.

steps.text: Grab the bar with both hands facing you, just wider than shoulder-width, you may need to jump or use a step
Pull yourself up with your back and biceps
Lower yourself back down carefully

guide.intro: The Clean is the strongest way to get a heavy weight to shoulder height and a fundamental movement in Olympic lifting.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on the floor
Push the bar off the floor like a deadlift
Drive with hip and toe extension while shrugging so that the bar reaches the highest point possible
Jump down under the bar and catch it on your fully bent arms at the top of your chest
Drive from the bottom of the squat until standing straight

guide.intro: The Clean and Jerk is a complex multi-stage Olympic movement to lift the heaviest weight possible above your head using a barbell.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on the floor
Push the bar off the floor like a deadlift
Drive with hip and toe extension
Jump under the bar into a low Front Squat position
Stand up straight
Spring down until there is a slight bend in your knees and back
Recoil up driving the bar from your feet into the air
Split your legs to get under the bar as it reaches its highest point
Extend the arms fully and catch the bar in a stable split position

guide.intro: Clean and Press combines the advantages of Power Clean and Shoulder Press into a single workout busting movement that develops your posterior chain and shoulders.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on the floor
Accelerate the bar off the floor like a Power Clean
Catch the bar on your shoulders and stand up
Press the bar overhead using a Shoulder Press

guide.intro: The Close Grip Bench Press is a great exercise for building upper body muscle, with a focus on increasing the strength and size of your triceps rather than your chest, unlike the regular Bench Press. It's an effective exercise for enhancing regular Bench Press strength.

steps.text: Lie on a flat bench
Grip the barbell with both hands as close as together as comfortable
Unrack the barbell and hold the bar directly above your shoulders
Lower the barbell to your lower chest without flaring the elbows out
Press the barbell back to the starting point

guide.intro: Crunches are an easy classic way for developing your abs

steps.text: Start on the ground with your knees bent at 90 degrees and your feet on the floor
Put your hands behind your head
Flare your elbows out to the side
Tense your core to curl your upper back off the floor
Lower back to the ground slowly

guide.intro: Deadlift is a fundamental hip hinge exercise to develop the ability to lift a heavy weight off the ground. Performing deadlifts increases whole body strength, confidence and can increase testosterone levels in the body.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on the floor
Grip the barbell with a hook or overhand grip
Look straight ahead and take the slack out of the bar
Lift the bar off the floor in a controlled manner
Stand up and lockout
Lower the bar down in a controlled manner

guide.intro: Decline Bench Press allows you to target your lower pecs more effectively than a conventional Bench Press using a decline bench to change the angle of the lift.

steps.text: Lie on a decline bench
Grip the barbell with both hands
Unrack the barbell and hold the bar directly above your shoulders
Lower the barbell to your chest
Press the barbell back to the starting point

guide.intro: Dips are an excellent way of training the chest and triceps using your bodyweight. You can make the movement more chest or tricep heavy by varying the angle.

steps.text: Step up to the dipping platform
Start with both arms extended fully in an upright position
Lower yourself down so that your body is angled more towards the ground until your arm forms a right angle
Push yourself back up to the starting position

guide.intro: Dumbbell Bench Press works the chest with heavy weight without having the risk of getting trapped under a heavy barbell. It also allows you to work each arm separately to prevent imbalances.

steps.text: Begin by sitting on the end of a bench with two dumbbells by your knees
Lie back until you are flat on the bench, keeping your arms bent
Press the dumbbells up until your arms are vertical
Lower the dumbbells until the handles are at chest height and repeat

guide.intro: Dumbbell Curls are a fundamental movement for growing the biceps. By turning the wrist you also work the brachialis which makes your biceps look fuller.

steps.text: Begin with two dumbbells by your side
Contract your first bicep and lift the dumbbell to upper chest height
Turn your wrist while lifting so that your inside hand faces you
Lower the dumbbell back down
Repeat with your other arm
Lower the dumbbell back down to complete the repetition

guide.intro: Dumbbell Fly effectively isolates the pectorals allowing you to work them through a full range of motion using a relatively light weight. The light weight can still provide a good stimulus because you hold your hands far away from the body.

steps.text: Begin by sitting on the end of a bench with two dumbbells by your knees
Lie back until you are flat on the bench, keeping your arms straight above you ready to start the movement
Lower the dumbbells until the handles are at chest height
Clench the dumbbells towards each other using your pecs until your arms are vertical

guide.intro: Dumbbell Front Raise allows you to isolate the front delts and work them hard using relatively light weights. You can lift the dumbbells together or alternately.

steps.text: Begin with two dumbbells resting in front of your hips with your hands facing your body
Raise the dumbbells up and forwards using your shoulders, until they reach shoulder level
Lower the dumbbells back to your hips

guide.intro: Dumbbell Lateral Raise is a challenging exercise that isolates the side delts, requiring them to work hard while using relatively light weights.

steps.text: Begin with two dumbbells by your side
Raise the dumbbells sideways away from your body, until they reach shoulder level
Lower the dumbbells back to your side

guide.intro: Dumbbell Rows allow you to focus on one arm at a time and work each side of your back to exhaustion. You may be able to lift more than Bent Over Row as this movement is more supported.

steps.text: Place two dumbbells on either side of a weights bench
Kneel on the bench with one leg, with the other leg to the side
Lean over the weights bench until your body is parallel to the bench
Support the body with the kneeling arm
Grab the dumbbell from the floor with the other arm and bring it to bench height
Pull the dumbbell towards your midriff until it touches you
Lower the dumbbell back to bench height

guide.intro: Dumbbell Shoulder Press works each shoulder/arm in isolation which helps address imbalances. You can also support your back using a bench, reducing the risk of injury and preventing cheating using bad form.

steps.text: Begin standing with two dumbbells at shoulder level
Press the dumbbells up with your shoulders, together, until your arms are straight, and they almost touch
Lower the dumbbells back down to shoulder height and repeat

guide.intro: Dumbbell Shrug is a more flexible version of the Barbell Shrug that allows the arms to be in a neutral position which can help isolate the trapezius more.

steps.text: Begin with two dumbbells resting by your sides
Lift your shoulders up as high as they go, without tensing your arms, keeping your arms close to your sides
Lower your shoulders back down until the bar is resting without any tension in your body

guide.intro: Dumbbell Tricep Extension works each triceps in isolation above your head, requiring you to use your core for stability. This provides a good crossover with other vertical pushing movements where your elbows go above your head.

steps.text: Begin with a single dumbbell by your side
Press the dumbbell above your head until your arm is straight
Hold your side with the other arm to increase stability, or use it to support your elbow to keep it in a stable position
Lower the dumbbell behind your head until your hand is at ear level, keeping the elbow in the same position
Raise the dumbbell back to vertical using your tricep

guide.intro: Front Squat is a more advanced variation of the Back Squat that puts extra emphasis on your quadriceps and requires more mobility, especially around the shoulders and wrist. The upright posture can help with injury prevention compared to Back Squat.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on a rack at shoulder height
Rest the barbell on your shoulders and cross your arms to hold it in place with your hands (you can also rest the barbell on your hands like a Power Clean)
Stand up to lift it off the rack
Step back with each leg until you are clear of the rack, setting up your footing for the lift
Lower yourself until your thighs are at most parallel to the floor
Raise yourself back up to standing

guide.intro: Good Morning is a traditional posterior chain builder for developing power in the legs and glutes.

steps.text: Begin with a barbell on a rack at shoulder height
Unrack the barbell and step forward so you can bend forward
Bend forward allowing your knees to bend slightly and keeping a stable spine position, feeling the load on the glutes and hamstrings (like a Romanian Deadlift)
Tense your glutes to return to the original position

guide.intro: Hack Squats are a great exercise to build leg muscle. They focus on the quads more than traditional squats and reduce the involvement of your lower back.

steps.text: Load the machine with weight plates.
Stand on the platform and set up your footing
Grip the handles and unrack the weight
Lower yourself until your thighs are at least parallel to the platform
Push yourself back up until your knees are almost locked out

guide.intro: Hex Bar Deadlift is a safer version of Deadlift that allows you to lift a similar weight completely vertically with neutral arms. It avoids the bar rubbing on your shins and focuses more on the front of the legs.

steps.text: Begin with the hex bar on the floor
Grip the hex bar with a neutral grip
Push into the ground and stand up while keeping your arms relaxed
Lower the hex bar back to the floor

guide.intro: Hip Thrust is a very popular posterior chain builder, used especially in sports for developing jump power. It also helps with injury prevention.

steps.text: Wrap a cushion around the middle of the barbell
Lie back on the side of a bench
Place the barbell above your hips
Keep your chin tucked and focus on one spot
Drive from the glutes until your shoulders form a line parallel to the ground with your knees
Return your hips to the ground

guide.intro: Horizontal Leg Press lets you stimulate your lower body with a large amount of weight. It keeps you safe as you can easily exit the machine after a failed rep without any help as you just let the weight stack return to the ground.

steps.text: Begin by selecting the weight on the machine
Sit on the machine and place your feet at hip-width on the footplate
Push your legs to full extension and release the handle
Lower the sled until the weight stack almost hits the ground and repeat

guide.intro: Incline Bench Press allows you to target your upper pecs more effectively than a conventional Bench Press using an incline bench to change the angle of the lift.

steps.text: Lie on an incline bench
Grip the barbell with both hands
Unrack the barbell and hold the bar directly above your shoulders
Lower the barbell to your upper chest
Press the barbell back to the starting point

guide.intro: Incline Dumbbell Bench Press works the upper chest with heavy weight without having the risk of getting trapped under a heavy barbell. It also allows you to work each arm separately to prevent imbalances and has good crossover other horizontal/vertical pressing movements.

steps.text: Begin by sitting on the end of an incline bench with two dumbbells by your knees
Lie back until your back is flat on the bench, keeping your arms bent
Press the dumbbells up until your arms are vertical
Lower the dumbbells until the handles are at chest height and repeat

guide.intro: Incline Dumbbell Fly effectively isolates the upper pectorals allowing you to work them through a full range of motion using a relatively light weight. The light weight can still provide a good stimulus because you hold your hands far away from the body.

steps.text: Begin by sitting on the end of an incline bench with two dumbbells by your knees
Lie back until your back is flat on the bench, keeping your arms straight above you ready to start the movement
Lower the dumbbells until the handles are at upper chest height
Clench the dumbbells towards each other using your pecs until your arms are vertical

guide.intro: Lat Pulldown is a primary mass builder for the upper back, similar to Pull Ups but making it much easier to vary the load compared to using your bodyweight.

steps.text: Select the weight on the machine
Grab the bar with both hands wider than shoulder-width
Sit down on the bench, keeping your arms extended and lifting the weight stack off the ground
Pull the bar down with your back and biceps until it reaches your chin
Return the bar to the initial position and repeat

guide.intro: Leg Extension is the primary exercise for working the quadriceps in isolation. It is an excellent way to develop the size and strength of the quads. You can target different heads by changing the angle between your feet.

steps.text: Sit on the leg extension machine and set up the seat and footrest
Select your weight
Ready your feet under the footrest
Push equally with both legs until your legs are straight
Lower the legs and stop before the weight stack hits the bottom before repeating

guide.intro: Dumbbell Tricep Extension works each triceps in isolation in front of your head on a bench. This provides a good crossover with other horizontal pushing movements where your elbows stay in front of your chest.

steps.text: Begin with a single dumbbell by your side
Lay back on a flat bench
Press the dumbbell above your head until your arm is straight
Support your elbow with the alternate hand to keep it in a stable position
Lower the dumbbell past your head until the dumbbell reaches ear level, keeping the elbow in the same position
Raise the dumbbell back to vertical using your tricep

guide.intro: Lying Leg Curl works the hamstrings in more isolation than Seated Leg Curl as you are unable to move your body forward to help with the movement because you are lying face first on the machine.

steps.text: Set up the seat, footrest and weight
Lie face-first on the leg curl machine
Ready your feet behind the footrest
Pull equally with both legs until your legs are at least 90 degrees
Return the legs to the start, stopping before the weight stack hits the bottom

guide.intro: Tricep Extension works the triceps in isolation in front of your head on a bench. This provides a good crossover with other horizontal pushing movements where your elbows stay in front of your chest.

steps.text: Begin on a flat bench with a barbell on your lap
Lay back on the bench
Press the barbell above your head until your arms are straight
Lower the barbell until it is just above your forehead
Raise the barbell back to vertical using your triceps

guide.intro: Machine Calf Raise allows you to fully exhaust your calf muscles using a significant amount of weight while leaving you in a safe position if you fail.

steps.text: Select your weight or add weight plates
Stand on the machine and put your shoulders under the shoulder pads
Lift the weight stack and stand vertical, then lower yourself down at the ankle
Extend your ankle until your feet are pointing straight down in line with your leg
Lower the ankle back down

guide.intro: Machine Shoulder Press removes the need to balance so that you can focus solely on improving your shoulder strength.

steps.text: Sit on the machine
Adjust the seat height to ensure proper alignment with your shoulders
Set the weight to match your current strength level
Grip the handles using a regular or neutral grip
Push upwards until your arms are fully extended
Gently lower the weight back down to neck height, maintaining control throughout the movement

guide.intro: Muscles Ups are a challenging combination of Pull Ups and Dips, normally using momentum to allow you to raise yourself above the bar further than you would do with a Pull Up.

steps.text: Grab the bar with both hands around shoulder-width, you may need to jump or use a step
Build some momentum to make the movement easier
Create space between you and the bar so you can rotate around it
Increase the momentum with your back and biceps and pull until your shoulders are above the bar
Rotate your hands around the bar as your shoulders move above it
Push the bar down and away with your triceps and chest until your arms are vertical
Lower yourself down, trying to keep momentum if doing another rep

guide.intro: Pendlay Row works the back through a longer range of motion than typical Bent Over Rows as your back is more horizontal to the floor and the bar comes to a stop on the ground every rep. This also helps with static strength.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on the floor
Grip the barbell with an overhand grip
Bend over until your body is horizontal to the ground
Pull the bar until it reaches your belly button
Lower the bar back and rest it on the ground

guide.intro: The Pistol Squat is a challenging variation of the King of Exercises that you can do anywhere. It requires significant strength, balance and practice.

steps.text: Start from a standing position
Begin with the left leg
Move your weight on to the left leg
Lift your right leg off the foor and keep it straight
Send your hips back, keeping your chest up and right leg straight
Lower yourself until your left thigh is least parallel to the floor
Raise yourself back up to standing

guide.intro: Power Clean develops serious sport-specific power and helps you become a better athlete by developing your posterior chain.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on the floor
Accelerate the bar off the floor like a deadlift
Continue past your hips and keep accelerating the bar upwards
Drop under the bar as it rises past your chest
Stand up supporting the bar on your shoulders/chest

guide.intro: Pulls Ups are excellent for developing a strong back and athletic physique. Getting to one pull up is a milestone in your strength training journey.

steps.text: Grab the bar with both hands wider than shoulder-width, you may need to jump or use a step
Pull yourself up with your back and biceps
Lower yourself back down carefully

guide.intro: Push Jerk allows you to lift more than a Push Press as you lower your body as you press instead of keeping straight, reducing the effort from the shoulders.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on a rack at shoulder height
Lift the barbell off the rack onto your shoulders with your hands under the bar
Spring down until there is a bend in your legs
Recoil back up from the hips
Push with your shoulders to fully extend the arms
Bend the knees to decrease the press distance
Straighten your arms and stop bending the knees
Stand up with the bar above your head
Lower the bar to neck height and repeat

guide.intro: Push Press allows you to lift more weight overhead than a standard Shoulder Press by using leg drive to accelerate the bar at the start of the movement. This additional weight allows you to increase the load and training stimulus on the body and shoulders.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on a rack at shoulder height
Lift the barbell off the rack onto your shoulders with your hands under the bar
Spring down until there is a bend in your legs
Recoil back up from the hips, ankles and shoulders
Push with your shoulders to fully extend the arms, with your feet flat on the ground
Lower the bar to neck height and repeat

guide.intro: Push Ups build the chest anywhere, anytime. You can vary the position to load the chest or triceps more.

steps.text: Begin on the ground with your arms and legs straight
Position your hands at shoulder-width
Lower yourself to the ground, keeping your core engaged
Press into the ground until your arms are straight

guide.intro: Romanian Deadlift focuses more on the posterior chain allowing you to develop the hamstrings/hips with lighter loads than conventional Deadlift. This makes it great for sports where you need to develop your posterior chain while avoiding injury.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on the floor or on a rack
Grip the barbell with a hook or overhand grip
Stand vertically with the barbell after using a conventional deadlift to get it off the floor, alternatively lift from a rack at knee/hip height and take two small steps back
Lower the bar below the knees by reversing your backside with your knees slightly bent, shoulders over the bar, holding tension on your hamstrings/glutes
Activate the posterior chain to lift the bar off the floor
Clench your glutes and stand up straight, allowing the bar to travel vertically

guide.intro: Seated Calf Raise isolates your calf muscles by putting the weight above your knees, cutting out the rest of the body. This allows you to focus completely on the contraction in your calf.

steps.text: Sit on the machine and set up the seat and footrest
Select your weight or add weight plates
Ready your feet on top of the footrest, allowing the weight to pull your ankles towards the ground
Extend your ankle until your feet are pointing straight down in line with your leg
Lower the ankle back down

guide.intro: Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press works each shoulder/arm in isolation which helps address imbalances. You can also support your back using a bench, reducing the risk of injury and preventing cheating using bad form.

steps.text: Begin with two dumbbells by the side of a bench set with a vertical back Lift the two dumbbells and sit back on the bench
Knee each dumbbell up to shoulder height
Press the dumbbells up with your shoulders, together, until your arms are straight and they almost touch
Lower the dumbbells back down to shoulder height and repeat

guide.intro: Leg Extension is the primary exercise for working the hamstrings in isolation. It is an excellent way to develop the size and strength of the hamstrings.

steps.text: Sit on the leg curl machine and set up the seat and footrest
Select your weight
Ready your feet in front of the footrest
Pull equally with both legs until your legs are at least 90 degrees
Return the legs to the start, stopping before the weight stack hits the bottom

guide.intro: Shoulder Press is a fundamental movement for building strong shoulders and arms.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on a rack at shoulder height
Lift the barbell off the rack and hold it at neck height
Press the bar overhead
Lower the bar to neck height and repeat

guide.intro: The Pistol Squat is a challenging variation of the King of Exercises that you can do anywhere. It requires significant strength, balance and practice.

steps.text: Stand up straight
Lift one foot and move your weight over to the other foot
Lower yourself until your bent thigh is at most parallel to the floor
Raise yourself back up to standing

guide.intro: Sit Ups work your core through a large range of motion providing a good workout in one exercise, covering your core and part of your hips.

steps.text: Start on the ground with your knees bent at 90 degrees and your feet on the floor
Put your hands behind your head
Flare your elbows out to the side
Tense your core to curl your upper back off the floor
Pull your torso off the floor with your hip flexors
Stop when your body is vertical
Lower back to the ground slowly

guide.intro: Sled Leg Press lets you use the full strength of your lower body to lift a huge amount of weight, and trigger muscle growth doing so.

steps.text: Begin by loading up the sled with weight plates
Lay back on the machine and place your feet at hip-width halfway up the sled
Push your legs to full extension and release the handle
Lower the sled until your knees are at least at a right angle
Push the sled back to full extension and repeat

guide.intro: The Snatch is a complex Olympic movement that requires flexibility, coordination and strength.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on the floor
Grip the barbell as wide as possible with an overhand grip
Drive with the hips lifting the bar like a Deadlift
Shrug the shoulders and extend the toes so that the bar reaches the highest point
Pull yourself under the bar and drop into an Overhead Squat position
Drive from the bottom of the squat and stand up straight

guide.intro: The Squat is the King of Exercises. It develops your legs more completely than any other exercise and also requires a solid core.

steps.text: Grip the barbell and stand up to lift it off the rack
Step back with each leg until you are clear of the rack, setting up your footing for the lift
Lower yourself until your thighs are at most parallel to the floor
Raise yourself back up to standing

guide.intro: Stiff Leg Deadlift focuses more on the posterior chain allowing you to develop the hamstrings/hips with lighter loads than conventional Deadlift. This makes it great for sports where you need to develop your posterior chain while avoiding injury.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on the floor
Grip the barbell with a hook or overhand grip
Activate the posterior chain to lift the bar off the floor
Clench your glutes and stand up straight
Lower the bar below the knees by reversing your backside, holding tension on your hamstrings/glutes

guide.intro: Sumo Deadlift is a wide stance Deadlift that targets the thigh muscles to a greater extent. Depending on your body type, the wide stance can reduce the range of motion allowing you to lift more weight.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on the floor
Position your feet wider than shoulder width
Grip the barbell with a hook or overhand grip
Look straight ahead and take the slack out of the bar
Lift the bar off the floor in a controlled manner
Stand up and lockout
Lower the bar down in a controlled manner

guide.intro: T Bar Row is a good alternative to Bent Over Rows for developing the upper back.

steps.text: Begin with the T Bar on the floor after loading it with plates
Bend over until your body is facing the ground
Grip the T Bar using the handle
Pull the bar until your elbows are at your side
Lower the bar back to the ground

guide.intro: Thrusters start from a full squat, increasing the contribution from the legs compared to a Push Press.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on a rack at shoulder height
Lift the barbell off the rack onto your shoulders with your hands under the bar
Squat down into a full squat position
Recoil back up from the hips
Push with your shoulders once standing to keep the bar moving upward
Stand with your arms straight above your head
Lower the bar to neck height and repeat

guide.intro: Tricep Extension works the triceps in isolation above your head, requiring you to use your core for stability. This provides a good crossover with other vertical pushing movements where your elbows go above your head.

steps.text: Begin with the barbell on a rack at shoulder height
Grip the barbell slightly narrower than shoulder width
Lift the barbell off the rack and hold it at neck height
Press it above your head until your arms are straight
Lower the barbell behind your head until it reaches just above the ears, keeping the elbows in the same position
Raise the barbell back to vertical using your triceps

guide.intro: Tricep Pushdown is a great alternative to Tricep Extension for isolating the triceps. Instead of pushing upwards against gravity you push downwards using a cable for resistance. This makes it a better accessory for Dips.

steps.text: Select the weight on the machine
Grab the handle with both hands using a close grip
Stand slightly away from the machine
Push the handle down with your triceps until your arm is straight
Return the handle to the initial position and repeat

guide.intro: Tricep Rope Pushdown is a variation on Tricep Pushdown where you use a rope as a handle, increasing the difficulty and changing the wrist angle.

steps.text: Select the weight on the machine
Grab the rope with each hand
Stand slightly away from the machine
Push the rope down with your triceps until your arm is straight
Return the rope to the initial position and repeat

guide.intro: Upright Row is a classic bodybuilding exercise for developing the trapezius and the shoulders, giving you the physique of a Greek statue. Some people avoid it due to shoulder impingement.

steps.text: Begin with a barbell or EZ bar on the floor or bench
Grip the barbell in the center with your hands close together
Stand up straight with the barbell with your arms extended
Lift the bar up to just below shoulder height
Lower the bar back to hip level
